speaker: ANTONIO
text: In sooth I know not why I am so sad.

speaker: ANTONIO
text: It wearies me, you say it wearies you.

speaker: ANTONIO
text: But how I caught it, found it, or came by it,

speaker: ANTONIO
text: What stuff ’tis made of, whereof it is born,

speaker: ANTONIO
text: I am to learn.

speaker: ANTONIO
text: And such a want-wit sadness makes of me

speaker: ANTONIO
text: That I have much ado to know myself.

speaker: SALARINO
text: Your mind is tossing on the ocean,

speaker: SALARINO
text: There where your argosies with portly sail

speaker: SALARINO
text: (Like signiors and rich burghers on the flood,

speaker: SALARINO
text: Or, as it were, the pageants of the sea)

speaker: SALARINO
text: Do overpeer the petty traffickers

speaker: SALARINO
text: That curtsy to them, do them reverence,

speaker: SALARINO
text: As they fly by them with their woven wings.

speaker: SOLANIO
text: Believe me, sir, had I such venture forth,

speaker: SOLANIO
text: The better part of my affections would

speaker: SOLANIO
text: Be with my hopes abroad. I should be still

speaker: SOLANIO
text: Plucking the grass to know where sits the wind,

speaker: SOLANIO
text: Piring in maps for ports and piers and roads;

speaker: SOLANIO
text: And every object that might make me fear

speaker: SOLANIO
text: Misfortune to my ventures, out of doubt

speaker: SOLANIO
text: Would make me sad.

speaker: SALARINO
text: My wind cooling my broth

speaker: SALARINO
text: Would blow me to an ague when I thought

speaker: SALARINO
text: What harm a wind too great might do at sea.

speaker: SALARINO
text: I should not see the sandy hourglass run

speaker: SALARINO
text: But I should think of shallows and of flats,

speaker: SALARINO
text: And see my wealthy Andrew docked in sand,

speaker: SALARINO
text: Vailing her high top lower than her ribs

speaker: SALARINO
text: To kiss her burial. Should I go to church

speaker: SALARINO
text: And see the holy edifice of stone

speaker: SALARINO
text: And not bethink me straight of dangerous rocks,

speaker: SALARINO
text: Which, touching but my gentle vessel’s side,

speaker: SALARINO
text: Would scatter all her spices on the stream,

speaker: SALARINO
text: Enrobe the roaring waters with my silks,

speaker: SALARINO
text: And, in a word, but even now worth this

speaker: SALARINO
text: And now worth nothing? Shall I have the thought

speaker: SALARINO
text: To think on this, and shall I lack the thought

speaker: SALARINO
text: That such a thing bechanced would make me sad?

speaker: SALARINO
text: But tell not me: I know Antonio

speaker: SALARINO
text: Is sad to think upon his merchandise.

speaker: ANTONIO
text: Believe me, no. I thank my fortune for it,

speaker: ANTONIO
text: My ventures are not in one bottom trusted,

speaker: ANTONIO
text: Nor to one place; nor is my whole estate

speaker: ANTONIO
text: Upon the fortune of this present year:

speaker: ANTONIO
text: Therefore my merchandise makes me not sad.

speaker: SOLANIO
text: Why then you are in love.

speaker: ANTONIO
text: Fie, fie!

speaker: SOLANIO
text: Not in love neither? Then let us say you are sad

speaker: SOLANIO
text: Because you are not merry; and ’twere as easy

speaker: SOLANIO
text: For you to laugh and leap, and say you are merry

speaker: SOLANIO
text: Because you are not sad. Now, by two-headed Janus,

speaker: SOLANIO
text: Nature hath framed strange fellows in her time:

speaker: SOLANIO
text: Some that will evermore peep through their eyes

speaker: SOLANIO
text: And laugh like parrots at a bagpiper,

speaker: SOLANIO
text: And other of such vinegar aspect

speaker: SOLANIO
text: That they’ll not show their teeth in way of smile

speaker: SOLANIO
text: Though Nestor swear the jest be laughable.

speaker: SOLANIO
text: Here comes Bassanio, your most noble kinsman,

speaker: SOLANIO
text: Gratiano, and Lorenzo. Fare you well.

speaker: SOLANIO
text: We leave you now with better company.

speaker: SALARINO
text: I would have stayed till I had made you merry,

speaker: SALARINO
text: If worthier friends had not prevented me.

speaker: ANTONIO
text: Your worth is very dear in my regard.

speaker: ANTONIO
text: I take it your own business calls on you,

speaker: ANTONIO
text: And you embrace th’ occasion to depart.

speaker: SALARINO
text: Good morrow, my good lords.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Good signiors both, when shall we laugh? Say, when?

speaker: BASSANIO
text: You grow exceeding strange. Must it be so?

speaker: SALARINO
text: We’ll make our leisures to attend on yours.

speaker: LORENZO
text: My Lord Bassanio, since you have found Antonio,

speaker: LORENZO
text: We two will leave you. But at dinner time

speaker: LORENZO
text: I pray you have in mind where we must meet.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: I will not fail you.

speaker: GRATIANO
text: You look not well, Signior Antonio.

speaker: GRATIANO
text: You have too much respect upon the world.

speaker: GRATIANO
text: They lose it that do buy it with much care.

speaker: GRATIANO
text: Believe me, you are marvelously changed.

speaker: ANTONIO
text: I hold the world but as the world, Gratiano,

speaker: ANTONIO
text: A stage where every man must play a part,

speaker: ANTONIO
text: And mine a sad one.

speaker: GRATIANO
text: Let me play the fool.

speaker: GRATIANO
text: With mirth and laughter let old wrinkles come,

speaker: GRATIANO
text: And let my liver rather heat with wine

speaker: GRATIANO
text: Than my heart cool with mortifying groans.

speaker: GRATIANO
text: Why should a man whose blood is warm within

speaker: GRATIANO
text: Sit like his grandsire cut in alabaster?

speaker: GRATIANO
text: Sleep when he wakes? And creep into the jaundice

speaker: GRATIANO
text: By being peevish? I tell thee what, Antonio

speaker: GRATIANO
text: (I love thee, and ’tis my love that speaks):

speaker: GRATIANO
text: There are a sort of men whose visages

speaker: GRATIANO
text: Do cream and mantle like a standing pond

speaker: GRATIANO
text: And do a willful stillness entertain

speaker: GRATIANO
text: With purpose to be dressed in an opinion

speaker: GRATIANO
text: Of wisdom, gravity, profound conceit,

speaker: GRATIANO
text: As who should say “I am Sir Oracle,

speaker: GRATIANO
text: And when I ope my lips, let no dog bark.”

speaker: GRATIANO
text: O my Antonio, I do know of these

speaker: GRATIANO
text: That therefore only are reputed wise

speaker: GRATIANO
text: For saying nothing, when, I am very sure,

speaker: GRATIANO
text: If they should speak, would almost damn those ears

speaker: GRATIANO
text: Which, hearing them, would call their brothers fools.

speaker: GRATIANO
text: I’ll tell thee more of this another time.

speaker: GRATIANO
text: But fish not with this melancholy bait

speaker: GRATIANO
text: For this fool gudgeon, this opinion.—

speaker: GRATIANO
text: Come, good Lorenzo.—Fare you well a while.

speaker: GRATIANO
text: I’ll end my exhortation after dinner.

speaker: LORENZO
text: Well, we will leave you then till dinner time.

speaker: LORENZO
text: I must be one of these same dumb wise men,

speaker: LORENZO
text: For Gratiano never lets me speak.

speaker: GRATIANO
text: Well, keep me company but two years more,

speaker: GRATIANO
text: Thou shalt not know the sound of thine own tongue.

speaker: ANTONIO
text: Fare you well. I’ll grow a talker for this gear.

speaker: GRATIANO
text: Thanks, i’ faith, for silence is only commendable

speaker: GRATIANO
text: In a neat’s tongue dried and a maid not vendible.

speaker: ANTONIO
text: Is that anything now?

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Gratiano speaks an infinite deal of nothing,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: more than any man in all Venice. His reasons are as

speaker: BASSANIO
text: two grains of wheat hid in two bushels of chaff: you

speaker: BASSANIO
text: shall seek all day ere you find them, and when you

speaker: BASSANIO
text: have them, they are not worth the search.

speaker: ANTONIO
text: Well, tell me now what lady is the same

speaker: ANTONIO
text: To whom you swore a secret pilgrimage,

speaker: ANTONIO
text: That you today promised to tell me of?

speaker: BASSANIO
text: ’Tis not unknown to you, Antonio,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: How much I have disabled mine estate

speaker: BASSANIO
text: By something showing a more swelling port

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Than my faint means would grant continuance.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Nor do I now make moan to be abridged

speaker: BASSANIO
text: From such a noble rate. But my chief care

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Is to come fairly off from the great debts

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Wherein my time, something too prodigal,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Hath left me gaged. To you, Antonio,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: I owe the most in money and in love,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: And from your love I have a warranty

speaker: BASSANIO
text: To unburden all my plots and purposes

speaker: BASSANIO
text: How to get clear of all the debts I owe.

speaker: ANTONIO
text: I pray you, good Bassanio, let me know it;

speaker: ANTONIO
text: And if it stand, as you yourself still do,

speaker: ANTONIO
text: Within the eye of honor, be assured

speaker: ANTONIO
text: My purse, my person, my extremest means

speaker: ANTONIO
text: Lie all unlocked to your occasions.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: In my school days, when I had lost one shaft,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: I shot his fellow of the selfsame flight

speaker: BASSANIO
text: The selfsame way with more advisèd watch

speaker: BASSANIO
text: To find the other forth; and by adventuring both

speaker: BASSANIO
text: I oft found both. I urge this childhood proof

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Because what follows is pure innocence.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: I owe you much, and, like a willful youth,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: That which I owe is lost. But if you please

speaker: BASSANIO
text: To shoot another arrow that self way

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Which you did shoot the first, I do not doubt,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: As I will watch the aim, or to find both

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Or bring your latter hazard back again,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: And thankfully rest debtor for the first.

speaker: ANTONIO
text: You know me well, and herein spend but time

speaker: ANTONIO
text: To wind about my love with circumstance;

speaker: ANTONIO
text: And out of doubt you do me now more wrong

speaker: ANTONIO
text: In making question of my uttermost

speaker: ANTONIO
text: Than if you had made waste of all I have.

speaker: ANTONIO
text: Then do but say to me what I should do

speaker: ANTONIO
text: That in your knowledge may by me be done,

speaker: ANTONIO
text: And I am prest unto it. Therefore speak.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: In Belmont is a lady richly left,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: And she is fair, and, fairer than that word,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Of wondrous virtues. Sometimes from her eyes

speaker: BASSANIO
text: I did receive fair speechless messages.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Her name is Portia, nothing undervalued

speaker: BASSANIO
text: To Cato’s daughter, Brutus’ Portia.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Nor is the wide world ignorant of her worth,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: For the four winds blow in from every coast

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Renownèd suitors, and her sunny locks

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Hang on her temples like a golden fleece,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: Which makes her seat of Belmont Colchos’ strond,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: And many Jasons come in quest of her.

speaker: BASSANIO
text: O my Antonio, had I but the means

speaker: BASSANIO
text: To hold a rival place with one of them,

speaker: BASSANIO
text: I have a mind presages me such thrift

speaker: BASSANIO
text: That I should questionless be fortunate!

speaker: ANTONIO
text: Thou know’st that all my fortunes are at sea;

speaker: ANTONIO
text: Neither have I money nor commodity

speaker: ANTONIO
text: To raise a present sum. Therefore go forth:

speaker: ANTONIO
text: Try what my credit can in Venice do;

speaker: ANTONIO
text: That shall be racked even to the uttermost

speaker: ANTONIO
text: To furnish thee to Belmont to fair Portia.

speaker: ANTONIO
text: Go presently inquire, and so will I,

speaker: ANTONIO
text: Where money is, and I no question make

speaker: ANTONIO
text: To have it of my trust, or for my sake.
